transcript.transcript: I am Susan Stacy interviewing Charles Hummel. Today's date is Wednesday, December 6, 2006. Wednesday December 6, 2006. This is part of the Julia Davis Park Memories Project celebrating the centennial of Julia Davis Park in 2007. I'm interviewing Charles at his home on Mobley Drive in Boise. Ways in which they can get a handle on this, particularly about improvements. And so, you know, the design competition for the improvements got created. Well, wait a minute, wait a minute. This happens to be one of my questions. Oh. Go ahead. No, I didn't mean to. It's the most, the question having to do with the most recent thing. But let's get to it. Did you join the committee? How did this come about, this competition? I was invited to join the committee, which is already in existence, and well over a year ago. The first meeting I went to, they said, we've been identifying improvements in the park. And we don't know. We're looking for ways in which to organize that project so that it would get some attention and would be a basis for raising funds to do these improvements. These are in addition to the Park Master Plan, which has already on record been adopted. So these are. So did you then, in response to those questions, come up with the idea of a competition? Yeah. Yeah, that's... So I... Somehow or another, the list of improvements got discussed and defined, at least in a general way. And then further discussions refined them down to the particular improvements that was the basis of the competition. And I suggested that they design competition. It compensated the design competition because that's the only time that I could improve or work with. And that I would help to write the specifications for the compensation issue. Assist with the development of the competition, which I did. And borrowed heavily from the competition experience that the CCDC has had for the river street improvement, the river street planner walkway plan. And that's what I'm talking about. So I wrote the plan. When you say it was compensated, you mean the participants? The participants would be compensated. By the Parks Department. Yes. And so the Parks Department put up a fund. And I can't remember now. It's very good. I can't remember how much was offered. But Of course, it involved putting out a notice, writing a description. All the projects were described. Oh, you see the book. We put out a notice, invited people to participate, created a jury, and promised the finalists that they would be compensated on a little bit of a thousand dollars a piece for their effort. We specified the product that they were going to produce. It was wood. And so... Why were you so interested in doing this? Well, I guess, first of all, I was just responding to a request from a number of friends. Diane Ronay and a number of other people kind of leaned on me and said, this group could use some assistance. Julianus Park is an interesting place. We were in all this. It's good helping with Julia Davis Park. So now I'm sitting in one of these meetings. I had never met the doctor before, so. Oh. Didn't have a, up until then, didn't have the slightest idea that Tom Davidson had this syndrome when we were around. So I know that now. Yeah, I know. I know. She doesn't know that now. There we go. So she's been around here for a while, or one way or the other, but I never knew her. There's not a huge amount of us going on. Huh. Well, let's go back and see. Well, that's how that came about. Yeah. But, you know, I've always been at Cedar Park. I lived in this area. Well, tell me about that. Where did you live? Remind me when you were born. 1925. 62135. Okay, where did you live? Well, I lived at 220 East Island Street. I was virtually at the end of Broadway Avenue, where St. Luke's Park is structurally developed. Well, not the big park structure, but the lower left corner of the avenue, I should speak. That's where I live. My house, by the way, got moved up on the avenue. A house moved up. My house. So, you know, you just take your bike and walk down Broadway Avenue, you're at the east end of Junior Davis Park. Although, when I was a kid, the east end of Junior Davis Park was up in Ellison, I'm the kid the East image of the area's part was on the elephant Known to be the part. Still was I have a recollection Gravel crusher gravel crusher, big wooden monster, and they're on on Myrtle Street and and the big gravel pond, which is now the lagoon on that end. That end of the park was still more or less wheat. But the gravel farm, the rock crusher was not in operation. There was an M.K. Rock crusher. M.K. Apparently had a lease or something to get gravel out of there. Because, you know, MK was doing a lot of contracting, locally, building. And the MK equipment yard and office buildings and everything were right there at Broadway and Myrtle. So anyway, I used to go down to the park. Well, it was the park. Well, the municipal park, too. But you didn't go to the municipal park for a while. You didn't have anything very interesting. And it was also kind of also had a lot of tourist in it. It was called the Municipal Tourist Park. And it was like for people to have trailers and come to town and have a chance to have a trailer. But you know, that was big stuff in Virginia. Well, not real big, but that was well before RVs That was a nice facility. The city didn't have available for tourists. But, Well, so what was attractive about that park? Julia Davis? Yeah. Well, it had all kinds of stuff. It had zoo, which is a good point. And band show, and a great playground. And, What do you remember about the playground? Well, it was the usual stuff in the swing. Two starters, and a merry-go-round swing. You know, I wasn't much into the playground. I had already outgrown the interest in the playground by the time I was going down to the park on my own. Do you remember how old you were before your parents let you go where you wanted? Where are you going out? Well, I always told them where I was going. And I was an only child. So my mother, in particular, was always very careful about what I was about to do. Well, where do you think you were? Nine years old. Eight, nine years old. Because I went to St. Joseph's School. And at first, my father used to drive me to school in the morning. And then I would walk. When school was out, I would walk down to his office and hang around the office. He would come home in the evening. And I'm sure that by the time I was, my recollection is by the time I was in the third grade, I was walking in the park. I'd walk. So by that time, you were also going on your own to the park? Yeah. And Then I got a bicycle, I can't remember when. And, you know, that tea's got to go. I'm going to go to... Still got caffeine? Yeah. Okay. So, the thing when I actually got a bike was, of course, you course, we had the whole city. But in the summertime, we used to go to the band concerts, and we used to do band concerts. And I knew people in the band, because Quite a few of the band members, in fact, I would think the majority of the band members also played in the Army National Guard band, which was headquartered for the 119th Engineer National Guard. And my father was a commanding officer in the 115th engineer. And so that was the other kind of part of my life that was a good trip in the future. And I went to summer camp, summer connection with our camp, as a striker, you know, making a flunk or two, making a polish or a skid, or a board kick, something like that. So the National Guard, you know, was coming to camp here in Europe. They were on Reserve Street. And it was quite a deal. There were two regiments, the regiment of engineers, the regiment of cavalry, the field artillery. That camp was really terrific. Did they do things at the park too? Did you guys play in there? Yeah, they would do exercise stuff at the park. So I'd build stuff on those ponds. The engineers would build pond field bridges and practice doing that at the park. And that was about the extent of the national park use of the palm. But in the wintertime, you know, the palms would freeze. We had colder winters in those days. They had kids with skate on them. And a kid was drowned falling through the ice. It was a basket. He was up on the roof of the Grove Street. And I can't remember which basket it was. It was a basket in the hall. I don't think it was the leather that week. But that was a terrible tragedy. And I was about 12 years old. I was just a child in the world. And I was, of course, going to St. Joseph's School. This kid was helping the high schoolers. He was like a high schooler. He should have been a kid. And I was going to St. Joseph's at that age. Of course, I was an altar boy at the cathedral. And the first big funeral that I was an altar boy serving at was that funeral. And, I think that was the first big Matt funeral I ever experienced. It was a tragedy. So when we went out to the cemetery, oh man, I thought it was just... In those days, the custom of course was the Lord would cast it. And that's the blessing we have. I remember the mother of this poor mother was just beside herself. She tried to jump into the grave. And he was trying to. That was the boy's house. Did you know the boy? No, I never knew him. I knew who the family was, but he was an older guy, a kid. He was a young adult, or maybe a senior in high school. I was in sixth grade or something, and I was in school. Whatever. After a while, you're an older boy, you've got kind of key targets, and you know. And you could become a sort of aficionado of funeral comparison. Well, did this tragedy in the park result in any changes in what happened? Oh, indeed it did. Oh, indeed it did. All the stations came on full roof over. And the result was that the city filled the pond to about three feet of depth. And also, the pond was maybe a little bit smaller. Well, maybe not that small. It was a little size. It was a big pond. And probably it would have filled in in the entire area. But the bottom was filled in so the water was only about three feet deep. And I think the result of that was, of course, it wasn't a free-flowing gravel pond anymore. And as we say, the water has to be let into the river and go through. In those days, it was like these rivers. So gravel ponds would have no water out, but they were always fresh. And the water would flow right here and actually. But that, then of course, I started ice skiing. I wasn't ice skiing. I wasn't allowed on that pond until that time. And actually, still, by a lot of kids, it became more popular to ice skate out here in the States. However, skating on a warm winter afternoon all by myself, I could have chanced a semi. I still could have been. I think I was hauled out because I couldn't break out. I couldn't get out. The water was up to my hips. I couldn't get out because every time I tried to get up on the ice with breaks when it falls. And Forest Service people across the smurdle saw what was going on and came up and threw a rope, came up with a rope and threw a rope and pulled it across the ice. There we are. Okay. There we are. OK. Are we making progress? Yes. It's old hands like your shoes. Ha, ha, ha. You're very gross. I know. Anyway, so that was it. And of course, I'd gone down on my bike. And it wasn't a terribly cold afternoon. And I'm going to find myself, I want to practice my, I want to get to be a better skater. And I've been sending one of the pair of skates, I don't know if they were in the Apple, shoes, but shoes, you know, it's two skates. Not the kind that you clamp on. There was this great informal game of hockey going on down there. So you know what they have to say about hockey. So I'm not sure that I told my mother that I was going to go down there. But I remember getting on my bike in the middle of the night and going up Broadway home, Sneaking in the house, getting out of my wet clothes before I get to Chicago. Ha, ha, ha. Ha, ha, ha. Ha, ha. Before Chicago. Yeah. Well, I love my mother. She was wonderful. She was a remarkable woman. If you're a single kid, you figure out ways. Mothers don't need to know. Every system. Mothers don't need to know some of the word things. Was the park, did the park have any kind of image in your mind as a place for kids, a place for me, a place for teens, a place for families, a place for grown-ups, or... I mean... Well, all of those groups used it. Right. And it was, of course, the attractions. You know, the blind were the only game in town for some of them. And the zoo, I look back on them now and I realize the zoo was pretty decremed. It was sad. It was a zoo, you know. But, you know, People would go down and look at the bears. Look at the bears. I don't know where else. To tell you the truth, I guess it's foxes. And there may have been one or two big animals, but I don't think the zoo got that out. Stuff like that. Well, were you interested as well in the airport, if you were in the park? Oh sure. I'd go across the bridge and visit the airport and watch the planes land. Because by that time, you know, Varney, United, I don't know anybody who was here, of course, was by my age, you know, old enough to go out. And the runway got light. That was a big deal. When the Capitol Boulevard Bridge was opened, what was that? 1934. I think it was 31. 31? Actually, I don't have that on my phone. It's in the early 30s. Yeah. But one of the things that you did in those days was you drove. You know, the family woulddrive. The family would get in the family car. And you'd drive around and admire things. And the new bridge was, of course, a great thing to see. And I remember going to the dedication parade of it, where I saw the parade. And you'd go across the bridge, The 9th Street University. Go across the 9th Street Bridge very slowly and admire the Chapel Boulevard Bridge, which is light. And then, of course, on Chapel Boulevard, or 9th Street, whichever street you were up now, you could admire at nighttime the lights on the runway, which is still a long way. The end of the runway was still a long way from Capitol Boulevard. I mean, that was quite a thing, to see the lights on the runway. I don't remember how I don't think it was a lot of my my flight Anyway, a lot of runway. Do you have photographs of you or your family taken in the park? No. None of them were in the park. Did your family picnic there? Occasionally, yeah. We weren't much for picnics. My mother was... My mother kept nature at a distance. She wasn't much for taking a blanket out and putting it on her. She was certainly a petrissant. But this is what we do. Yeah, we take lunches down there. And particularly when the band was playing, we'd go to a band concert. So I loved the band concerts. And my father enjoyed them, too. He was brilliant. Because he knew all those people in the band, and I liked the concerts. And these band concerts were regular events? Well, the city bands. On Sunday? Sunday afternoon. Sunday afternoon. And at least in the month of July and August. I would guess there were maybe five or six percent of the county on the answer list, which would be the bandit part. And did they attract a big crowd? Oh, a lot of people would come down there and sit up grass. That little slope in front of the bandstand there now is later. So I tell them, we just sat on the flat ground. It pulled up the tension. Aside from falling through the ice, did you ever get into any other kind of trouble in the park? That you care to mention? I played the tennis on the tennis courts, which were clay courts. And The city worked real hard to keep it in the top. So those tennis courts have been there a long time. They're larger now, of course. They're more court than they were, but the same location. And I think Steve Appleton feels the city's demise is more important than that. I'm not sure how he read that. I think they had a competition play there. Yeah, yeah, there was. And in fact, there was some, not really grandstands, but there were some benches that you could sit and watch the players. One court there along the end. And the other public tennis courts were at Public School Field. Our home is off of Wilmston Avenue. But I don't think it was where East Junior High was. There may have been other tennis courts in the field by the city. Possibly in Elm Grove Park. Those are the ones that are working. So really, it's boys' high school, 17, practice, of course, is the one area of the world that I have on one side. And on the part, too. Well, Do you remember whether boys and girls hung out at the park? Or was the park the way you did? Or was it mostly boys? You know, I don't remember anything in particular of interest to the girls down there. Neither do I. And that was before they were renting rowboats and stuff like that. Mm-hmm. That came a little later. Well, maybe girls weren't allowed to be quite as free roamers. That's an old issue, yeah. So you don't remember girls? Of course, the art galleries got built. Yeah, pretty early in the 30s, about the middle 30s. Yeah. You might want that here. Yeah, I have the Warrenston tradition,

lines.line: # Introduction and Context
> Susan Stacy interviews Charles Hummel as part of the Julia Davis Park Memories Project, aimed at celebrating the park's centennial in 2007.
** Introduction, Susan Stacy, Charles Hummel, Julia Davis Park, centennial, Memories Project **
keywords: ** Introduction, Susan Stacy, Charles Hummel, Julia Davis Park, centennial, Memories Project **

lines.line: # Committee Involvement and Design Competition

> Charles discusses his invitation to join a committee dedicated to park improvements. They brainstorm the idea of a design competition to organize the project and raise funds, in alignment with the existing Park Master Plan.
** committee, design competition, park improvements, Park Master Plan, fundraising **
keywords: ** committee, design competition, park improvements, Park Master Plan, fundraising **
